dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for a breathing problem at --Address Redacted--.
Hi, I'm Emily. I'm a paramedic with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what made you call 911 today?
I'm having trouble breathing, it's been getting worse all day.
Can you open your eyes for me?
Ugh, yes. It's hard to concentrate with how short I'm breathing.
What is your name?
My name is Sarah. I'm 35.
Where are we right now?
[CITY]. I'm having a bad asthma attack.
Can you squeeze my finger on my left hand and then my right?
Yes, no problem, really.
Can I do a quick check of your airway? It appears to be clear and patent.
Airway is clear and patent.
Let me listen to your lungs. You might notice some wheezing, especially in the upper parts.
Breathing is labored. Respiration rate is approximately 24 per minute. Equal chest rise.
I hear wheezing in both upper lobes. You don't have pain in the chest, right?
No pain, but it's hard to catch my breath.
Pulse is 118. It's pretty strong. Skin is warm, dry, and looks normal.
BP is 135 over 92. No signs of significant bleeding.
Can you tell me about your medical history?
I've had severe asthma since I was a kid. I've been having it really bad for the last week.
Have you used anything like albuterol to try to help today?
Yes, like multiple times, nothing helps.
Have you ever been intubated before?
Yes, three times. I needed ECMO after a really bad attack in 2018.
Any drug use lately that could have influenced this?
I used to use cocaine pretty often, and it made my asthma worse. But not today.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how would you rate your breathing difficulty right now?
I'd say about an 8. I can't sit still, otherwise, it feels harder to breathe.
Does lying flat or any specific position make it worse?
It gets better when I'm upright, like right now.
Can I take a quick look at your skin? It seems normal, warm, and dry.
Partners, can I have a quick look at her pupils?
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light. Nothing's out of the ordinary in the head and neck area.
I've already noted some wheezing in the upper lobes. Let's see if that changes with the treatment.
Pulse remains steady at 118.
Respirations began at 24 and dropped to 20 after treatment.
Blood pressure is 211 over 53.
Oxygen saturation is at 95%, trending up from 94%.
Patient is in sinus tachycardia. Completing 4-lead monitoring per protocol.
Blood glucose was not collected during this encounter.
I'm administering a Duoneb with Albuterol (5mg) and Atrovent (0.5mg) via Hand Held Nebulizer with a filter, per protocol.
We're also setting you up on auxiliary oxygen at 6 liters per minute now.
Attempting IV insertion in the left forearm with 22G needle. Unfortunately, it didn't go in.
We're now giving you Terbutaline IM 1mg to help with bronchodilation.
Injection is via IM in the proximal lateral thigh as per standing order.
Our protocol indicates respiratory distress-asthma exacerbation, so we're following the respiratory distress path.
Rechecking vitals-heart rate is still at 118, respirations at 20.
No adverse reactions noted. Wheezing is stable and treatment is ongoing.
Rechecking oxygen levels. Maintenance at 95% and stable.
Central O2 is now 94%. We'll continue to monitor closely.
We're transporting to [COMPANY_NAME] for continued evaluation and care.
Can you move up onto the stretcher on your own?
No problem at all.
Patient is secured on the stretcher and ready for transport.
We have a 35-year-old female with a history of severe asthma having an exacerbation.
Treatments included Albuterol/Atrovent nebulizer, oxygen at 6 LPM, and Terbutaline IM.
No allergies reported. Blood pressure showed a systolic spike to 211/53 on record.
IV started was unsuccessful-22G left forearm.
She transitioned to the hospital bed on her own without EMS help.
All procedures followed per protocol. No complications during transport.
She answered questions clearly and remained A&O.
Final vitals: 211/53 mmHg, 118 bpm, 20 rpm, 95% O2 with treatment.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia as recorded.
She reported feeling unwell for the past week and has no known drug allergies.
Secondary exam: Skin is normal, lung wheezes present, no thoracic pain.
Patient takes albuterol, Atrovent, fluticasone, and metoprolol for asthma.
O₂ improved breathing and is now on N/C at 2 LPM. Nebulizer administered first.
No spine immobilization. No reported pain or need for analgesia.
She remains neurologically intact with no signs of decline or secondary issues.
She has had three prior intubations and required ECMO in 2018 for asthma.
Oxygen saturation is trending up since initial assessment.
Pre and post ekg monitored for sinus tach. No arrhythmic changes.
She declined recent drug or ETOH use and has not used for this exacerbation.
We've followed protocol for respiratory distress with no deviations found.
Hospital was notified en route. No surprise findings at the scene.
She is ambulatory and transferred herself to the stretcher.
Weight is recorded as 91 kg. No fluid reported in last 24 hours.
Patient is 35F with no history of pregnancy or diabetes to mention.
No rashes, ulcers, or open wounds noted in secondary exam.
Numbness, tingling, or sensory issues were absent in extremities.
Abdominal exams showed no pain, rigidity, or masses located.
Initial blood pressure of 135/92, now 211/53 during reassessments.
IV was not started but is documented as attempted and done for record.
She spoke clearly, had no trouble, and is alert on arrival.
Signed transport form voluntarily without issue.
Encuentro concluded safely and per protocol.
